Of course it’s cheap, it’s mass manufactured crap and pushed out to stores in a matter of days after the idea has left the ‘designer’s’ desk. 104 times a year. Fast fashion is affordable at the expense of everything else. There’s very little from Zara that is 1) well constructed, 2) flattering, 3) comfortable to wear.I don’t mean this to be classist. I think society these days has developed an idea that having lots of clothes is ‘normal’ — for the vast majority of human history, clothes have been incredibly expensive and coveted items which were expected to last you a lifetime. Even rich people understood that and wore their clothes to death. Most people would own two, maybe three, full outfits. They would alter them to fit the fashion of the day, repair them when they got worn, give them away when no longer useful, and reuse them as rags once they got ruined. Maybe we need to go back to that — spend good money on a small collection of high quality clothes that fit your body and learn basic sewing to make it work for you. I’ve been wearing and altering clothes I’ve had for the last ten years and sewing holes in my ripped shirts so I don’t have to buy new. There is no reason whatsoever to go to Zara with any frequency. End rant.

An understanding of fabrics (how they wear, how they fall, how they should be maintained and cleaned), and an understanding of what cuts (lines, shapes) of clothing flatter your body best are good tools to have when going out to shop for clothing - whether that be in Zara's or Grieder. The Duchess of Cambridge recently wore a flattering, fitted double-breasted red blazer at the Euros Championship that was gorgeous, and she bought it at Zara's! The store doesn't matter as much as your knowledge of what looks most flattering and what does not flatter your figure. Too tight for form-fitting clothing on someone who is busty, or has a tummy, or who has large thighs is less flattering to them then something a little looser fitting, like the beige, empire cut shirt you tried on. For someone who has a big bust or tummy she wants to disguise, pair that with slim, cropped pants and a lovely pair of sandals or cute sneakers, and add a Loewe type straw bag, or a fancy shopper and you have a chic, casual look for someone who is heavier set, but still wants to look classy, elegant. In my opinion, a fool proof way to look expensive is the cut and fabric, and the knowing of how to mix more expensive pieces with less costly ones - like Kate, the Duchess of Cambridge did.

Fast fashion brands make clothes so they go out of style, lose shape or fall apart quickly. This forces you their customer to buy more clothes more often. In order for these companies to get richer, they have to keep this twisted cycle going. Selling cheap clothes equals more profits.There are many parallels between the fast fashion and fast food industries. They both create cheap products that fuel addiction. They have little concern for their workers or the environment.That $9 blouse may be cute, but it’s made from cheap fabric. And it’s so trendy you’ll only be able to wear it a few times. Will it fall apart or go out of style first?Either way, you lose. The blouse goes in the trash and you go back to the store to buy another top. The fast fashion cycle of destruction rolls on.

Here is a hack for east coast Americans: Those Zara stores will have a mix of US and EU tags when they get a new delivery. Example a blue dress will be marked 99 euros and the same blue dress will be marked for 99 dollars. However, the blue dress with the EU tag will be made of silk while the same blue dress with a US tag will be made of polyester. Buy the EU tag because silk is better than polyester and the cashier generally converts the 99 euros into 99 dollars so you’re not paying more for quality. You’re welcome!

Okay but like, fast fashion rlly sucks. Of you buy from fast fashion, it is definitely important to be extremely selective and make sure you buy something only if it is flattering, made to last and adds to your wardrobe otherwise you'll be buying something that is never going to be worn or won't last you. Also like, fast fashion is just plain bad for the environment so the less the better. Buying 2nd hand may make you look cheap, but if you look for quality materials and garments that are flattering and hopefully don't look too worn out, it would still probably be better than most fast fashion and better for the environment. Otherwise, fair trade designers that put time and effort into making fairly priced and sustainable garments are the way to go if you have money. They are more likely to be higher quality anyway. Besides, buying lots of cheap stuff vs buying less expensive stuff is about the same for your wallet anyway.

For those with a small budget, I recommend buying at thrift stores in or near upscale neighborhoods. Sure, there's a lot that's not worth buying, but sometimes you find a treasure piece that has been well constructed in exquisite materials. So, step 1: thrift. Step 2: find a tailor. I bought a wool and cashmere vintage coat for $9 and took it to the tailor, who shortened the hem and sleeves and took the waist in. He charged me $65 for the alterations. Spending 65 dollar to fix a coat that cost 9 seems stupid, until you consider that for less than $75, I now have a most scrumptious camel coat, lined in silk, with tortoiseshell buttons, that fits me like a glove. Speaking of gloves, cashmere and leather gloves, leather belts, wool hats, and the occasional silk top or skirt can be found at second hand stores for a tenth of what they would cost if they were new, and many times they are. I cannot tell you how many "new with tags" items I have purchased! You save money, are kinder to the environment, and curb the human exploitation and trafficking often associated with fast fashion by buying pieces that would otherwise end in a landfill.

Mish_elle and sea breeze: Thrift shops do not exist to sell to the poor. Thrift stores exist to take the castaways from the upper class and sell it to the middle class so those profits can be, in turn, applied to social services for the truly disadvantaged. Underprivileged people don't rely on shopping at thrift shops, they rely on the aid (including free clothes, meds, and furniture) purchased with funds made through those charity stores. When was the last time you saw a chronically homeless person buying Frye boots at a thrift store? And even if that happened occasionally (believe me, it doesn't) the overabundance of items at the store is an indication that there's always more supply than demand. No one is taking these things from the back of the poor. There's not a charity shop in the world that is ever understocked of inventory! There is so much excess that over 70% of clothing never gets sold directly to customers--it gets donated to "developing countries" (which is a horrible thing, given that most clothes are made precisely in those countries, but the fruit of their toils returns to them almost as if it were trash...this constant influx of donations also disrupts the local economy and devalues the cultural significance of regional textile producers, prints, art, weavers, etc.) or it gets compressed and sold by weight to be shredded and used as wall insulation, sofa/mattress/pillows/toys and other types of stuffing, industrial mats and heat protectants, rugs, athletic shoes and sportswear, pet bedding, baby car seats, absorbent items such as towels, etc. The best, best, BEST thing would be actually to consume less all together, but when one MUST buy something, thrift shops can be a good compromise since one is purchasing an item that won't end up in a landfill or shredded to make yet another shopping bag (the irony of it all!) while at the same time one's contributing to the funding of social services geared toward those who cannot shop---there or elsewhere.

I would a million times prefer to buy a tote bag, shoes, a dress, or a belt made by an artisan than an assembly-line item produced en masse for the cheapest price possible to get the largest mark up they can snatch. It is incredibly elegant to have a piece that has been custom made or tailored specifically for you. It's one of a kind, it's been made to last, it has a story, you probably know the person or family that designed and produced it. That's so much more elegant than carrying a purse with a logo that screams, "I would like to buy ________ but I can only afford this _________ bag that was made by 6 year olds in Tibet, just so I can feel a little fancy!" Try it. Get something made for you and when people compliment you for it, say, "Oh, yes, it was bespoke, from a little shop in _____________." BAM! Instant "oooooos" and "aaaaahhhhhs". Small and slow production, that is the definition of luxury! As I heard it explained once, nobody bats an eye about spending 5 month's salary on a wedding dress that they'll wear for 3 hours, but most people bristle at the idea of investing a good chunk of money on clothes they hope to wear on repeat, for years. That just doesn't make sense. Do we want to look good only for 3 hours...or do we want to be known for being elegant, always?

Anna, I don't know how familiar you are with brands/stores in the US, but could you include some of those. I live in Portland Oregon and NYC is not an option. We have Macys and Nordstrom. Also I see online brands that look intriguing. Are any of those worthwhile?

I also live in the US, and I shop at an online second store called ThredUp. There are good brands there, at huge discounts. I’ve been buying their stuff for 4 years now, and only 3 garments were not a good quality or fit. Some clothes can be returned, and you must invest some time to peruse their collections. It takes some time, but it is worth it. Most clothes also have a description of the quality: like new, minor pilling, minor defect, etc. I bought stuff with minor defects, and I could’t find the problem. If it’s minor pilling, it can be shaved with an electric fabric shaver. I usually type in a favorite brand, let’s say I type in Calvin Klein, and I pick my sizes. If I see something nice, I hit the “like” button. After a few days of liking items, I go to the favorites section, and look through everything I like to see the measurements, status, fabric, defects, etc. You don’t have to shop in one sitting. Some items get sold in the meantime, but that is why I like a lot of them, so I can have a good selection.

Hello Ladies,I usually get inspired from many fashion designs, I save interesting garments I see online or in Pinterest and change things based on my choice.I bring my own fabric to my tailor/ seamstress, (so I can assure the quality of the fabric) most of the time I listen to their opinion since they know how the fabric works the best.My tailor draws the sketch in front of me while we discuss, if I have a picture of the design which inspired I share if with them. (It’s sounds like a big process but it’s not) I don’t like to have same design in two colours (but it’s a personal choice). Like you mentioned it is efficient to get it like that. Hope this is informative. ️

This was an extremely important documentary. As a supporter of regenerative fashion it hit many of the right notes for me, except I don’t think it did even remote justice to what in my humble opinion is the greatest threat to the world after AMR (Anti-Microbial Resistance): Plastics / Microplastics / Nanoplastics. [Yes climate change is a massive threat, but a potentially resolvable one with the right political and social will, I still see no pathway for AMR & microplastics which are in everything from vehicle tires to paint to clothes].This Planet A video should have spent at least a minute to go into more granular details of why plastics in clothes are so bad. The short graphic that touched in this subject was really cool, but it did not really explain to the average viewer just how dangerous these micro (later turned into nano plastics) are for the entire global ecosystem including humans (more than 90% of taps across the world have microplastics; it’s in our air and it’s in our rain). And it’s causing havoc to the human endocrine systems and to all life on earth. And it’s not going anywhere, it will be there for thousands and thousands of years, and poor countries with consistently growing spread out populations means that it can never be collected. Maybe Planet A should do a documentary just focused on microplastics and nanoplastics, because this is the stuff that is rapidblh taking Planet A to the point of no return.

lived in Brazil up to my 30s, and buying clothes there, even fast fashion brands like zara, is very expensive (example: zara is considered a brand for high-middle class to rich people there. not extreme luxury but a luxury brand). So I grew accustomed to only buying clothes when I needed and on special occasions (birthday, christmas, church/family/social events). I also always shared clothes with my younger sister, my mom and one of my grandmas. Mom and grandma always had a full wardrobe, and often grandma would pull up some vintage piece out of her closet, or would keep our clothes that were out of fashion in our eyes, just so we would pick it up again a few years later. Clothes that didnt fit anymore or were not 100% , but still usable, we would give away to other people, at least twice a year, and clothes that were too old or damaged would turn into cleaning cloths or my other grandma would cut them make clothes for our toys/barbies/dolls. Now I live in a rich country where clothing, specially these fast fashion brands are much more affordable, and yes I do get tempted sometimes to buy stuff.. but I always try to remember myself that it makes much more sense to spend more in fewer pieces of clothing that are better quality and will last longer.. and I prefer to save my money for when I do NEED to buy something, so I can spend more in a better quality thing.

One of my methods for eco clothing is to target underwear and splurge on that every year around Christmas because it’s amongst the cheapest types of eco clothing vs eco t-shirts or jeans etc. plus it’s a practical buy and most importantly underwear and socks etc. are the clothing that wears out the most. So it’s an easy win for supporting eco products without worrying too much about buying more than you need. Plus a good way to help people dip their toes in the water, maybe they aren’t into going in on eco clothing but they might be into a good pair of wool hiking socks aka nice thickly cushioned wool work wear socks. Granted I would recommend looking at the type of socks they like before buying, or buying one pair or multiple pairs of different types to see what people like, before buying them a weeks worth of the preferred type since it’s amazing just how complicated buying socks for multiple people with multiple different preferences and needs can get. XD eg. Stay away from slow drying bamboo socks if someone is going to be wading through a wet paddock in the morning dew or people preferring short socks vs long socks and uniform colour requirements etc. etc.

Kinda related to a few ideas in this thread (relating to both self esteem/body image/multiple pairs of jeans! ) - I still have so many pairs of jeans in a variety of sizes from over the years, due to my size and shape changing. I lost a lot of weight 5 years ago from a combination of new medication side effects & some drawn out dental surgery dramas affecting what I could eat, so I had to buy a whole load of new clothes that actually fit (particularly things like jeans/trousers/bras/things that aren’t as flexible on sizing). Since then, the environmental conditions affecting the size and shape of my body changed in the complete other direction (hello pandemic lockdowns, and no longer walking so much on a daily basis as part of my commute!) that not only do the smaller clothes I bought no longer fit, but the ones I still had from before that time don’t either! ‍️By this point I feel like I could open my own secondhand clothes store with things in a variety of sizes, including stacks of jeans in great condition. I’m terrible at organising myself/logistics enough to properly sell all the things mounting up around me, from clothes to electronics (thanks ADHD!), and I’m literally at the point of thinking about whether I can pay someone to do it for me, so I can be free of both the physical things taking up valuable space and the guilt from not wanting to to do damage to the environment by creating more waste, and then have the mental peace and clarity from not feeling so encumbered by all this stuff. (I do also donate things to various charities as well as giving away via local apps, but the financial cost of all the things I’ve accumulated over the years that I now need to replace isn’t nothing, so I definitely have a mental resistance to zeroing out that value and then having to find the money all over again. But this is probably a me problem, and the amount of mental clarity and physical space I could regain is probably worth sucking it up…) And even after all that, the fact is I still don’t have any jeans that fit me, so I still need to obtain more clothes! For a handful of years I’ve been doing my best to avoid anything with polyester and preferring natural fabrics where possible (feeling sweaty while wearing it is a more immediately noticeable problem than the environmental impact, but that’s certainly a driving factor for me too). I’m prepared to spend more money on good quality clothes that are ethically made, with sustainable materials that do less damage to the environment and make me feel more comfortable when wearing them, but I’m finding it to be a real challenge to meet all of these criteria, and also be available in plus sizes, and in long lengths. I don’t care about being “fashionable” or following trends, just finding clothes I like that fit me well and feel comfortable. It wouldn’t change a lot of people’s behaviour, but having knowledge of and access to suitable alternatives instead of the ever-present fast fashion stores would be really helpful. It’s a bit like the psychology behind breaking/forming habits - knowing something is “bad” is absolutely half of the battle, but unless you know and have something to replace it with, you’re much less likely to be successful.(On that note, if anyone has recommendations for places like this to shop in the UK, or resources containing other lists/recs, I’m all ears!)

the marketing strategies of many companies in recent years have contributed to the development of beliefs centered around the pursuit of wealth, financial independence, and the acquisition of increasingly better and newer goods. True prosperity lies not only in personal wealth but also in the preservation of our natural environment and the welfare of fellow human beings. I am from an era that people wore classics in fine wools and cashmeres, cotton, linen, silk. Many of us had servants who maintained our clothing or we took it to a dry cleaner or laundry. My fathers dress shirts for work all went out to a Chinese laundry. My grandmother used a laundry/dry cleaners that came to her home to pick up and then delivered back cleaned clothing, sheets, and even towels.Clothes lasted MANY years...only womens underwear was washed by hand at home and the maid did that. My family was "comfortable" not multimillionaires.Of course I learned to care for clothing myself eventually and to clean my own home. Now that I am retired though I have servants again..abroad. These are considered a big step up from hand laboring in the fields. We have two servants...it sure beats having todo tropical gardeni g by my 79 yr old husband and me having to mop tile floor in a 4,000 sq ft home...and clean two story windows on a laddder I never could do. Our employees have worked for us15 yrs and are now middle class HERE. They own a car and a truck a big step up from bicycles they used to use! One of them now owns 2 houses ...decidedly middle class here!

The reason why SheIn is so successful is because of several reasons... One big one is that China is the importer of the world. They make so many products and they have realized this and took advantage. They are well equipped as a result of being the largest importing country in the world. They also have very cheap labor... The cost of making the items at a mass scale is also still very low. They have the power to make massive quantities for low labor costs and unbeatable prices. In the fashion industry, fashion companies create items in bulk and pay the factory. Then, they sell to fashion buyers wholesale price that sell the items in retail stores which is marked up again to consumers so that they can profit. They have totally skipped this part so they have made the item at the cheapest price possible. The price on their website is unbeatable because it's the price before wholesale value and before retail value. It's closer to the bulk value from the factory. They have skipped several steps in the supply chain management process.

The medical technology to check a whole body for skin cancers has really kicked up in the last decade. A good first step is to find a Skin Check Clinic or clinician who is recommended. Confirm the costs and who covers them. I believe the clinician hovers a scanning tool, with like an infrared light, across your skin. It takes medical images. They check spots you would never have thought of, or can't see yourself. Such as your scalp, behind your ears, your lips, tops of your feet, between your toes even! The service they offer is to provide a base assessment of your skin. If a mole appears to be growing, changes colour or becomes lumpy, they have an original file to check those changes against. Good luck with that!

​That'd be outside my scope for coverage in this video, but I can point you towards recent reporting on this issue from my colleagues that might be a good place for you to start:

personally feel that the main drawback for brands like Zara, compared to others like Lululemon or Patagonia or tentree, is that Zara doesn't leverage buzzwords such as "diversity," "sustainable," "organic," "care for Mother Earth," "fair pay," and "mental health." These terms allow other brands to significantly mark up their prices from manufacturing costs and attract customers by portraying themselves as superior. Cause most of the brands in the world have manufacturing in Asia (and we all know the conditions)Do you think Zara's next step could be to adopt this strategy to become more relevant? Essentially, engaging in a bit more "greenwashing," as this seems to resonate with the majority of consumers and social organizations?
I'm afraid that's not something I could speculate on here, sorry!
Ya did great Was there anything in the creation process you found difficult or unexpectedly fun?
Were they (Zara) quite open to you documenting various aspects of the business or were the any elements that were off limits? Also, what is your process from conception to filming?

​ We had to seek permission to film within the parts of Zara HQ and its distribution centres that we needed for this piece, but that's a fairly standard procedure. The interviews are all shot first, then I'll have a script in draft form prior to additional on-location filming. That script informs the shot list that's created for our producers and camera crew to tick off as they capture the footage.
Why do women buying fast fashion contribute so much to global warming ?

Thank you for the video. I am documentary film student myself and I was wondering how "the standard procedure" looks like in big production studios to produce videos like this? Like at the moment I have to do everything from asking permissions to film - to post production all by myself...

​ Firstly, good luck with the studies! But to your point: if you look at the end of video you'll see the end credits are quite extensive. The producer handles setting up interviews, filming locations, schedules and making sure everything is being completed on time; a supervising producer assists numerous producers to do that, filling in gaps as necessary. As the writer, I devise what our story should be, research questions, ask those on camera (you'll hear me briefly in this video doing so) and then take all the material away to write up the first version of a script. If there's a guest I'd like to include, the producer goes out to try and book them. The script goes through many revisions during the edit as we incorporate additional footage (see a reply to an earlier question on that point), fact-checking and other reporting. We also have a separate team putting together our graphics and animations alongside this to support the spoken elements, but that's reliant on me and the producers having identified and gathered the data we want to show. This is a key part of the process for Bloomberg because we have access to a vast amount of industrial and financial data through the Bloomberg Terminal; you'll see data, charts and visualisations are key features in all of these videos. But this is just a snapshot of the process. For this particular video, about 20 people were involved from multiple countries. I hope that's helpful.

You can't blame people for buying inexpensive clothes which end up in the bin when multiple companies are setting up to provide it. Where there is a demand, there will be a supply. Zara and H&M are just 2 in a deluge, none of whom would be there if there was no market!Also, they provide work for many people in third world countries. The conditions are not bad, even if the low wages represent what's common in those countries. We need to ask what the alternative is - for many of these workers, mainly women, would not be able to get jobs and would have to fall back on the oldest profession. This indeed happened when there was an outcry on teenage workers hired by a British firm. They got rid of the young workers, who had no choice but the street! A documentary made people aware of this. Now many firms provide education, childcare and other benefits. Whatever the environmental arguments, they are bringing jobs to areas where there are few available.

I think most environmental concerns is a subconscious burden we put on women. Think of all the plastic reduction, fast fashion waste all that burden is put on women. Women are simply easy target. Cooking, sorting out the fridge, grocery shopping, buying clothes and paying for them women are taking the most responsibility. The use of plastic bags and buying cheaper and stylish clothes were all ways to make women's life convenient and financially livable. Now we are just trying to suffocate them. Why don't we attack luxury cars and mid-scale cars, or electronic devices? These are bad too. Men in the long run always have an advantage, while women reproductive years and youth are limited so I think as a woman we shouldn't refrain ourselves to buy stuff that we want. No one should dictate us how we spend our money if that money was from hard work, or from years of academic hard work. Spending money to be eco-responsible we are trying to make women feel that they are unethical if they spend on non sustainable makeup or looking pretty or cleaning products for house chores. We are not like granny homemaker with a garden and full of glass jars era. Being eco friendly is time consuming , add mental load on the working women. The fact that shopping bags are non plastic just cheap paper ruin our shopping experience. You have this burden that the bag could break at anytime when you get home. There is no thought of people taking the commute and are not driving. They do not own cars or drive which is a plus to the environment yet they are penalized because that have to stress how many reusable bags to bring, look like a bag hoarder when going grocery shopping. It's added stress on commoners mostly the female counterpart. Zara form personal experience produces very chic coats and still at affordable price. I enjoy going to Zara to explore the trend it 's like going to the museum. I still have all my coats from Zara and still buying. Why a woman needs so many coasts well I do not drive so I need different coats for different weather conditions, for going to work, and look stylish at the same time. Why I need to be stylish? I need to navigate in society and I still need people's respect so I need to dress in a way to have the minimum respect to get to my end. When I become a retired senior I can wear cheap looking clothes because I'll get a pass as a senior anyways. But when you're in between and childless or single you only got yourself. So I think as long as we earn money legally we can spend the way we want as long as it is legal. Now with those environmental concern it pushes me to spend what I like as soon as possible before society strips me from my freedom with all this sustainable discussion. Let's all get penalized to make it fair with a communist policy and ration clothing. Let's all wear the same clothes, own same amount of clothes then. Finding a happy medium and promoting awareness I get it but in the end it's on women. Try to tell men not to buy his dream car he is going to throw a revolution. But women they would't be able to impose that threat to society to protect their own interests. So as long as luxury cars exist let me enjoy my Zara, Shein or Temu.

​ We had to seek permission to film within the parts of Zara HQ and its distribution centres that we needed for this piece, but that's a fairly standard procedure. The interviews are all shot first, then I'll have a script in draft form prior to additional on-location filming. That script informs the shot list that's created for our producers and camera crew to tick off as they capture the footage.

Thank you for the video. I am documentary film student myself and I was wondering how "the standard procedure" looks like in big production studios to produce videos like this? Like at the moment I have to do everything from asking permissions to film - to post production all by myself...

​ Firstly, good luck with the studies! But to your point: if you look at the end of video you'll see the end credits are quite extensive. The producer handles setting up interviews, filming locations, schedules and making sure everything is being completed on time; a supervising producer assists numerous producers to do that, filling in gaps as necessary. As the writer, I devise what our story should be, research questions, ask those on camera (you'll hear me briefly in this video doing so) and then take all the material away to write up the first version of a script. If there's a guest I'd like to include, the producer goes out to try and book them. The script goes through many revisions during the edit as we incorporate additional footage (see a reply to an earlier question on that point), fact-checking and other reporting. We also have a separate team putting together our graphics and animations alongside this to support the spoken elements, but that's reliant on me and the producers having identified and gathered the data we want to show. This is a key part of the process for Bloomberg because we have access to a vast amount of industrial and financial data through the Bloomberg Terminal; you'll see data, charts and visualisations are key features in all of these videos. But this is just a snapshot of the process. For this particular video, about 20 people were involved from multiple countries. I hope that's helpful.

Selective outrage is incredibly frustrating right now. People are outraged at Disney for partnering with a Middle Eastern company to establish a theme park—a licensing deal similar to what they have in China. Yet, those same individuals seem unfazed by the fact that an internment camp exists just across the park. This pattern of selective outrage isn’t limited to entertainment; it extends to the fashion world as well. Consumers continue to buy knockoffs and appropriated designs from smaller designers simply because they are cheaper, despite the ethical implications. The fashion industry is inherently toxic; people crave glamour and want it at a low price. Ironically, the very individuals who complain about these issues often do so while using their iPhones, showing little concern for the sweatshops behind their devices. And let’s not forget the celebrities who lecture us about environmental issues while traveling in private jets—classic limousine liberals and champagne socialists. It’s worth noting that actual scientific data reveals a more nuanced picture than what the media often portrays. For instance, we now have 20% more greenery on the planet because trees thrive on carbon. Yet, conversations around this vital information remain largely absent from mainstream discourse.

“On 24 October, Palestinians called for the boycott of the retail clothing chain Zara after its Israeli franchise holder hosted far-right Knesset member (MK) Itamar Ben Gvir for a campaign event.Canadian-Israeli dual national Joey Schwebel, chairman of Israel’s Zara line, hosted the anti-Palestinian MK for a campaign event a week before the upcoming elections on 1 November.Ben Gvir, the head of the far-right nationalist party Otzma Yehudit, translated as Jewish Power, will be running Tel Aviv’s legislative elections.Israeli news channel 12 exposed the meeting on 20 October, after which Palestinians within the occupied territories launched online campaigns urging for the boycott of Zara products.Videos surfaced on social media, showing Palestinian burning clothes from the retail store, calling on “fellow Palestinians to boycott this racist company.”Ben Gvir, was seen in a video on the evening of 13 October, wielding a pistol in the occupied east Jerusalem neighborhood of Sheikh Jarrah and urging settlers and police to shoot at Palestinians who throw stones at the occupation forces.Later trying to justify his behavior via Twitter, Ben Gvir said: “The politicians are tying the hands of our cops … It cannot be that Arabs throw stones next to cops and the cops don’t respond with fire.”Ben Gvir is also known for praising extremist settler Baruch Goldstein, who massacred 29 Palestinians at the Ibrahimi Mosque in 1994.On 23 October, opposition leader Benjamin Netanyahu remarked that Ben Gvir could be a minister of his cabinet on the condition that the former premier forms a government following the elections, despite declaring the far-right MK as “unfit” for a ministerial designation.Ben Gvir’s Religious Zionism party currently stands at 14 seats in the elections, while the National Camp party of Defense Minister Benny Gantz is at a mere 12 seats.”

When my family first came to Japan, we saw UNIQLO as a cheap store for basics, but it got more and more expensive over time in the last 20 years, while our income dropped. Now, we don't go there every month to buy something good, but once a season.H&M and Zara was good in the 2010s with a lot of sales and we'd go to Nagoya for them. Even Old Navy had good sales every time. But, as always, the Japanese mindset turns to national brands and stores during hard times and, while Uniqlo and GU has grown, western clothing brands stopped being affordable with higher prices on cheap clothes.

Many are complaining about the high price and low quality of Zara. I think those judgements are fundamentally wrong. The global fashion industry is extremely extremely well organized and delivers to every customer the exact optimized product and the exact price point. When you want a high quality completely European made men's dress shirt you have to pay around 300 - 450 €. Who will afford this for every day? US made Jeans are in the same range. There was a question from another commentator, why people don't give away their old clothes? I can answer this. Nobody wants them. Not even for free. When you have a high quality 200€ children parka it's really difficult to sell it.

I’m a loyal customer of Zara, and I can sum up why Zara feels almost untouchable.In 2023, Egypt faced a major dollar shortage that disrupted imports. Most clothing stores struggled shipments were delayed, and collections didn’t arrive on time. Zara, however, managed to launch its full winter collection right at the start of the season.As a result, Zara sold through its entire collection while other brands were still catching up. By the time competitors finally stocked their items, winter was already over, and they missed their chance.Call it great marketing, an exceptional supply chain, or sharp market intuition! the outcome is the same.That’s what builds a loyal customer.Trust is what creates loyalty.
